title: browser-use
oneLiner: Open-source browser automation for AI agents.
summary: browser-use is a browser automation project that gives agents a practical way to inspect pages, take actions, and complete web tasks.
whyItMatters: The browser is still where a huge amount of work happens. Browser automation turns agents from chat interfaces into systems that can operate real web workflows.
notFor: Workflows that cannot tolerate browser fragility
seoTitle: browser-use: Open-source browser automation for AI agents
seoDescription: A concise editorial profile of browser-use, including what it does, who it is best for, license, repo, and related open-source AI resources.
shareDescription: Open-source browser automation for AI agents.

coreStrengths.title: Browser actions for agents
coreStrengths.description: browser-use gives agents a way to inspect pages, understand page state, and perform browser actions as part of a task.
coreStrengths.whyItMatters: Many useful workflows still live behind web interfaces, so browser capability lets agents operate where APIs do not exist.

coreStrengths.title: Task-oriented web automation
coreStrengths.description: The project is designed around agents completing web tasks, not just replaying fixed browser macros.
coreStrengths.whyItMatters: That makes it more relevant for dynamic sites where the next action may depend on what the model sees.

coreStrengths.title: Open-source skill building block
coreStrengths.description: It can be treated as a reusable browser skill inside broader agent workflows.
coreStrengths.whyItMatters: Builders can test browser automation without starting from a blank automation stack.

useCaseNotes.title: Web task agents
useCaseNotes.description: Use it when an agent needs to navigate sites, fill forms, or gather information from pages.

useCaseNotes.title: QA-style workflows
useCaseNotes.description: A fit for browser checks where the task requires both page interaction and model judgment.

useCaseNotes.title: Prototype agent skills
useCaseNotes.description: Useful for testing whether a web workflow can become an agent-operable skill before hardening it.

compareNotes.title: Choose it when browser state matters
compareNotes.summary: If the workflow has a reliable API, direct integration may be cleaner. browser-use is more relevant when the browser is the real interface.

compareNotes.title: Expect browser fragility
compareNotes.summary: Like most browser automation, it should be evaluated against site changes, authentication, rate limits, and reliability expectations.

thumbnailBrief.visualMotif: browser window with modular command blocks
